abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are evolving from passive observers into active agents, solving problems through Visual Expansion (invoking visual tools) and Knowledge Expansion (open-web search). However, existing evaluations fall short: they lack flexible tool integration, test visual and search tools separately, and evaluate primarily by final answers. Consequently, they cannot verify if tools were actually invoked, applied correctly, or used efficiently. To address this, we introduce Agentic-MME, a process-verified benchmark for Multimodal Agentic Capabilities. It contains 418 real-world tasks across 6 domains and 3 difficulty levels to evaluate capability synergy, featuring over 2,000 stepwise checkpoints that average 10+ person-hours of manual annotation per task. Each task includes a unified evaluation framework supporting sandboxed code and APIs, alongside a human reference trajectory annotated with stepwise checkpoints along dual-axis: S-axis and V-axis. To enable true process-level verification, we audit fine-grained intermediate states rather than just final answers, and quantify efficiency via an overthinking metric relative to human trajectories. Experimental results show the best model, Gemini3-pro, achieves 56.3% overall accuracy, which falls significantly to 23.0% on Level-3 tasks, underscoring the difficulty of real-world multimodal agentic problem solving.

citations.fullCitation: Xinhai Hou, Shaoyuan Xu, Manan Biyani, Moyan Li, Jia Liu, Todd C Hollon, and Bryan Wang. Codev: Code with images for faithful visual reasoning via tool-aware policy optimization. arXiv preprint arXiv:2511.19661, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper is cited as a direct inspiration for Agentic-MME's core methodology of process verification. Agentic-MME adopts and extends CodeV's approach to evaluate the faithfulness of intermediate visual artifacts, moving beyond simple final-answer scoring to ensure tools are used correctly.
citations.title: Codev: Code with images for faithful visual reasoning via tool-aware policy optimization

citations.fullCitation: Xingang Guo, Utkarsh Tyagi, Advait Gosai, Paula Vergara, Jayeon Park, Ernesto Gabriel Hernández Montoya, Chen Bo Calvin Zhang, Bin Hu, Yunzhong He, Bing Liu, et al. Beyond seeing: Evaluating multimodal llms on tool-enabled image perception, transformation, and reasoning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.12712, 2025.
citations.justification: VisToolBench is a representative benchmark that focuses specifically on 'Visual Expansion'—the use of image manipulation tools. Agentic-MME is positioned as an advancement over such works by integrating visual tool use with a core open-web search component to evaluate their synergistic combination.
citations.title: Beyond seeing: Evaluating multimodal llms on tool-enabled image perception, transformation, and reasoning

citations.fullCitation: Shilong Li, Xingyuan Bu, Wenjie Wang, Jiaheng Liu, Jun Dong, Haoyang He, Hao Lu, Haozhe Zhang, Chenchen Jing, Zhen Li, et al. Mm-browsecomp: A comprehensive benchmark for multimodal browsing agents. arXiv preprint arXiv:2508.13186, 2025b.
citations.justification: This benchmark is relevant as it addresses the 'Knowledge Expansion' aspect by evaluating multimodal web browsing. Agentic-MME is designed to fill a gap identified in such works by introducing a rich suite of active visual manipulation tools and verifying their intermediate outputs, thus unifying deep visual interaction with web retrieval.
citations.title: Mm-browsecomp: A comprehensive benchmark for multimodal browsing agents

citations.fullCitation: Romain Froger, Pierre Andrews, Matteo Bettini, Amar Budhiraja, Ricardo Silveira Cabral, Virginie Do, Emilien Garreau, Jean-Baptiste Gaya, Hugo Laurençon, Maxime Lecanu, et al. Gaia2: Benchmarking llm agents on dynamic and asynchronous environments. arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.11964, 2026.
citations.justification: GAIA2 is a significant generalist agent benchmark that also highlights the necessity of process-aware evaluation. This citation is important because it contextualizes Agentic-MME within the broader research trend of creating more rigorous agent evaluations, showing how Agentic-MME contributes by applying this principle specifically to the multimodal visual and search domain.
citations.title: Gaia2: Benchmarking llm agents on dynamic and asynchronous environments

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "Agentic-MME: What Agentic Capability Really Brings to Multimodal Intelligence?".

---

### Research Report: Agentic-MME: What Agentic Capability Really Brings to Multimodal Intelligence?

#### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by a collaborative group of authors from multiple institutions:
*   Qianshan Wei (CASIA, UCAS)
*   Yishan Yang (SEU)
*   Siyi Wang (SEU)
*   Jinglin Chen (SEU)
*   Binyu Wang (NJU)
*   Jiaming Wang (NJU)
*   Shuang Chen (UCLA)
*   Zechen Li (SEU)
*   Yang Shi (PKU)
*   Yuqi Tang (BUAA)
*   Weining Wang (CASIA)
*   Yi Yu (NTU)
*   Chaoyou Fu (NJU)
*   Qi Li (CASIA)
*   Yi-Fan Zhang (CASIA)

Qianshan Wei, Yishan Yang, and Siyi Wang contributed equally to this work. Qi Li and Yi-Fan Zhang are the corresponding authors.

#### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have advanced from passive systems that interpret static inputs to active "agents" capable of interacting with their environment to solve problems. This shift towards agentic capabilities involves two primary dimensions: "Visual Expansion," where models actively manipulate and analyze images (e.g., cropping, enhancing) to reveal hidden information, and "Knowledge Expansion," where models leverage open-web search to access and verify external facts.

Prior to this work, existing benchmarks for evaluating multimodal agentic capabilities exhibited several limitations. These included a lack of flexible and comprehensive tool integration, often treating visual tools and web search as separate modules rather than integrated components. Consequently, the synergistic interplay between visual and knowledge expansion in complex, real-world scenarios remained largely unexplored. Furthermore, most evaluations primarily focused on the correctness of the final answer, offering limited insights into the underlying process—such as whether tools were correctly invoked, accurately applied, or used efficiently. This outcome-based assessment made it challenging to diagnose specific failure modes, conflating issues of perception, tool execution, and strategic planning. Agentic-MME addresses these gaps by introducing a process-verified benchmark designed to holistically evaluate the synergistic application of visual and knowledge expansion in agentic MLLMs.

#### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop a comprehensive and process-verified benchmark to assess the "agentic capability" of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), particularly focusing on how these capabilities contribute to multimodal intelligence. The authors identified three critical shortcomings in existing evaluation methodologies that motivated the creation of Agentic-MME:

1.  **Lack of Flexibility and Comprehensiveness in Tool Integration:** Existing evaluations often separate the use of visual tools from open-web search, treating them as independent functionalities. This fragmentation limits the assessment of an agent's ability to fluidly select and switch between diverse visual and search tools within a unified framework. The motivation was to create a benchmark that integrates both types of tools, enabling agents to coordinate them for more complex problem-solving scenarios.
2.  **Unexplored Synergy Between Visual and Knowledge Expansion:** The majority of existing benchmarks do not feature realistic scenarios that specifically test the combined and interdependent functioning of visual and knowledge expansion capabilities. The authors argue that the true value of an agentic MLLM is realized in "intertwined" tasks that cannot be solved by either capability in isolation. Therefore, a key motivation was to design tasks that necessitate deep coupling and iterative interaction between manipulating visual information and retrieving external knowledge.
3.  **Absence of Rigorous Process Verification:** Current evaluations predominantly focus on the correctness of the final answer, providing insufficient insight into the intermediate steps an agent takes. This approach makes it difficult to ascertain if tools were actually invoked, whether they were applied correctly (e.g., cropping the intended region), or if they were used efficiently. The motivation was to move beyond final-answer grading by implementing a detailed, process-level verification mechanism that audits fine-grained intermediate states, thereby enabling diagnosis of specific failure modes.

To address these motivations, Agentic-MME introduces tasks that require active image manipulation to extract localized cues, followed by the coordination of these cues with open-web retrieval for factual verification or knowledge acquisition. The benchmark aims to provide a diagnostic tool to pinpoint whether agent failures stem from issues with individual capabilities or from the complex coordination required for higher-order multimodal reasoning.

#### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology employed in developing Agentic-MME focuses on creating a process-verified benchmark to evaluate multimodal agentic capabilities.

**Benchmark Design and Task Setup:**
Agentic-MME comprises 418 real-world tasks across 6 domains and 3 difficulty levels, designed to assess the synergy between visual tool use and open-web retrieval. Each task includes one or more images and a question. Agents interact with a unified tool-augmented interface providing 13 distinct visual operations (e.g., crop, rotate, enhance, grayscale, blur, edge_detect) and 4 open-web retrieval tools (google_search, google_lens_search, fetch_webpage, download_image).

**Task Scenarios and Difficulty Levels:**
Tasks are stratified into three difficulty levels based on interaction complexity:
*   **Level 1 (Visual Expansion Focus):** Tasks requiring a single decisive visual operation, such as a single crop or rotation, to extract the answer.
*   **Level 2 (Level 1 + Knowledge Expansion):** Tasks involving multi-step workflows that combine visual manipulation with open-web search, typically solvable within three interaction rounds (e.g., crop → rotate → web-search).
*   **Level 3 (Synergistic Coupling):** Challenging scenarios demanding iterative, interleaved execution of both visual and search tools. These tasks involve integrating multiple scattered clues, advanced CV analysis (e.g., frequency-domain transformations), or resolving severe visual ambiguity through explicit hypothesis-verification loops combining visual hints with multi-hop web searches for cross-validation.

**Metrics:**
The evaluation utilizes accuracy (Acc) for final answers, supplemented by process-aware and efficiency metrics derived from stepwise checkpoints:
*   **S-axis:** Measures the fraction of passed checkpoints related to Knowledge Expansion, assessing search-related strategies, keyword relevance, and intermediate answer correctness.
*   **V-axis:** Measures the fraction of passed checkpoints related to Visual Expansion, further split into:
    *   **V_tool:** Checks if the agent initiated the required visual tool use at the correct step.
    *   **V_true:** Verifies if the generated intermediate visual artifact genuinely contains the required evidence.
*   **Overthink:** Quantifies efficiency by penalizing redundant behaviors compared to human minimal trajectories, defined as `max(0, C_agent - C_human) / (C_human + 1)`, where `C` counts interactions.

**Data Collection and Annotation:**
A controlled protocol (Fig. 2) ensures rigorous and verifiable process-level supervision:
*   **Image Sourcing:** High-resolution, visually complex images across 6 domains were curated, intentionally requiring diverse visual toolkits (e.g., oversized images for cropping, low-light for enhancement).
*   **Model-in-the-Loop Backward Drafting:** Annotators used a state-of-the-art MLLM (e.g., Gemini 3 Pro) to identify visual details overlooked or hallucinated by passive inspection. Visual manipulation tools were then applied to isolate evidence, and the MLLM's perception of the processed image was cross-checked against human ground truth to ensure active manipulation was a prerequisite.
*   **Granular Step-wise Annotation:** Full human reference trajectories were meticulously annotated. For each step, annotators recorded: (1) natural-language intent, (2) specific tool/Python operations, (3) intermediate visual artifacts with test questions and expected answers for V-axis verification, and (4) search keywords, human-verified URLs, and expected answers for S-axis verification.
*   **Answer Standardization:** Questions were drafted to yield concise, verifiable answers, with explicit prompt instructions for final format to enable deterministic and regex-friendly scoring.

**Quality Control and Assurance:**
Each instance underwent human-model co-verification:
*   **Human Verification:** Instances were solved by authors and two independent verifiers, resolving disagreements and auditing step-wise annotations for accuracy and logical soundness.
*   **Model Verification (Step-wise Oracle Testing):** State-of-the-art models were guided through human reference paths, receiving ground-truth intermediate observations at key steps. Tasks were retained only if the model reached the correct final answer, confirming the underlying evidence was perceptible and failures reflected planning/execution issues.

**Unified Tool Interface and Execution Harness:**
The benchmark supports two interaction modalities:
*   **Atomic Tool Calls (Atm mode):** Models interact via structured function-calling APIs.
*   **Sandboxed Code Execution (Gen mode):** Models write sandboxed Python code for visual transforms.
The harness normalizes outputs into a unified event stream. For code-writing agents, an AST-based tracer extracts canonical visual operations from executed code traces for consistent scoring. Retrieval requests are cached for determinism and replay. Evaluation uses an MLLM-as-a-Judge approach (gpt-4o-mini) for intermediate visual artifacts (V_true) and search checkpoints (S-axis).

#### 5. Main Findings and Results

The evaluation of various open-source and closed-source multimodal models on Agentic-MME revealed several key findings:

1.  **Significant Performance Gap with Humans, Acute on Level-3 Tasks:** Human solvers achieved an overall accuracy of 93.8% and 82.3% on Level-3 tasks. In contrast, even the best-performing model, Gemini 3 Pro (Atm mode), reached only 56.3% overall and 33.3% on Level-3 tasks. Without tools, Gemini 3 Pro scored 7.5% on Level-3, improving to 33.3% with full tool access, indicating the necessity of tools for complex tasks. However, a substantial gap (33.3% vs. 82.3% on L3) persists, suggesting current agents struggle with multi-step planning and reliable tool execution for real-world workflows.

2.  **Open-Source Models Lag in Search and Planning:** Closed-source models consistently outperformed open-source counterparts across all difficulty levels. This disparity was particularly evident in Level-3 tasks, where open-source models like Qwen3 VL-235B achieved 10.1% and Thyme-rl dropped to 2.5% accuracy. Analysis of the S-axis (strategy and tool execution for knowledge expansion) indicated that models like Thyme-rl and Deepeyesv2 had S-scores below 5%, signifying severe failures in formulating search queries and extracting useful intermediate information. This suggests that while open-source models may invoke tools, they lack the robust retrieval planning necessary for multi-step problem-solving.

3.  **Structured Tool APIs Outperform Code Generation in Current Implementations:** Across all evaluated models, the Atomic (Atm) mode, using structured function-calling APIs, matched or exceeded the performance of the Code Generation (Gen) mode. For instance, GPT 5.2 in Gen mode showed low visual tool invocation (V_tool ≤ 7.6%), resulting in an overall V-score of 10.4%. Switching to Atm mode raised V_tool above 70% and overall V to 58.1%. This suggests that code generation imposes a higher cognitive burden on models, requiring them to manage syntax, I/O, error recovery, and program composition. However, the Gen mode, as seen with Gemini 3 Flash (47.7% overall), also demonstrated potential for competitive results, indicating that bridging the reliability gap while preserving the flexibility of code generation remains an open challenge.

4.  **Eager Tool Invocation Often Yields Incorrect Results:** Some models demonstrated a propensity for aggressively invoking tools without producing accurate outputs. For example, Thyme-rl (Gen, L1) achieved a V_tool of 63.3% but a V_true of only 13.0%, and Qwen3 VL-32B-think (Gen, L1) had V_tool of 66.4% against a V_true of 9.7%. This indicates frequent misapplication of visual tools, such as cropping incorrect regions, leading to misleading artifacts and wasted interaction budget. In contrast, models like Qwen3 VL-235B (Atm, L3) showed closely aligned V_tool (48.8%) and V_true (48.4%), suggesting more reliable tool parameterization. Agentic-MME's process-level checkpoints facilitate pinpointing these specific failure points.

**Further Analysis:**
*   **Visual Grounding:** Removing all images resulted in near-zero accuracy (e.g., Gemini 3 Flash: 2.63%), confirming that tasks genuinely require visual evidence and are free from data leakage.
*   **Active Tool Use:** The "Full (Img+Search)" setting consistently outperformed "Perception-only," "Image-only," and "Search-only" settings for both Gemini 3 Flash and Qwen3 VL-235B, validating that Agentic-MME necessitates both Visual and Knowledge Expansion. Level-3 tasks, in particular, showed super-additive gains when both capabilities were combined, confirming the synergistic design.
*   **Stepwise Annotations Quality:** Providing models with oracle guidance (+Visual Cues and +Stepwise Guidance) led to consistent performance improvements, especially for stronger models, validating that the human trajectories capture actionable planning structures. However, performance did not saturate to near-perfect scores, indicating the challenge of continuous autonomous execution even with perfect blueprints.
*   **Overthinking Behavior:** Models systematically "overthought" relative to human trajectories, with Atm mode generally being more efficient than Gen mode. DeepeyesV2 showed low tool utilization (OT=0) and low accuracy (22.5%), while GPT-5-mini over-explored (12.13 calls/task) for only 33.5% accuracy. Gemini 3 Pro achieved the highest accuracy by balancing exploration with reliable execution.

**Fine-Grained Error Analysis:**
Analysis categorized failures into seven modes:
*   **Reluctance to act:** Models frequently resorted to passive guessing instead of actively manipulating images (up to ~50% of errors).
*   **Overthinking Collapse:** Stronger agents were prone to redundant tool-invocation loops.
*   **Unfaithful Visual Tool Use:** Models often cropped irrelevant regions or produced noisy artifacts.
*   **Tool-Misexecution:** Frequent syntax errors in Gen mode were largely eliminated by structured Atomic APIs.

#### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

Agentic-MME represents a significant contribution to the evaluation of multimodal large language models by moving beyond traditional outcome-based assessments to a process-verified methodology. Its primary significance lies in its capacity to systematically diagnose the strengths and weaknesses of MLLM agents in complex, real-world problem-solving scenarios.

The benchmark's design, which integrates active visual manipulation (Visual Expansion) with open-web retrieval (Knowledge Expansion) within a unified framework, is crucial for developing agents that can handle "intertwined" tasks where neither capability alone is sufficient. By stratifying tasks into three difficulty levels, particularly emphasizing synergistic coupling in Level 3, Agentic-MME pushes the boundaries of evaluation to assess higher-order reasoning and iterative hypothesis verification across modalities.

The introduction of granular, human-annotated stepwise checkpoints and dual-axis evaluation (S-axis for strategy/knowledge and V-axis for visual evidence) is a key innovation. This allows for fine-grained auditing of intermediate tool intent, the faithfulness of visual artifacts, and execution efficiency through the "overthinking" metric. This level of detail provides a diagnostic roadmap, enabling researchers to pinpoint precisely where and why models fail—whether due to poor planning, incorrect tool parameterization, or an inability to interpret processed evidence.

The empirical findings expose a critical gap between frontier models and human performance, especially in complex, multi-step workflows. The observations regarding the struggles with reliable multi-step planning, unfaithful tool execution, and redundant exploration (overthinking) highlight specific areas for improvement in current MLLM architectures. The comparative analysis between structured API (Atm) and code generation (Gen) modes also offers insights into the trade-offs between reliability and flexibility in agent implementation.

The potential impact of Agentic-MME is multifaceted:
*   **Diagnostic Tool:** It serves as a rigorous diagnostic tool for identifying bottlenecks in multimodal intelligence, guiding the development of more robust and reliable agents.
*   **Benchmark for Advanced Research:** It provides a challenging benchmark for advancing research into long-horizon multimodal agents capable of sophisticated intertwined visual and knowledge reasoning.
*   **Improved Agent Development:** By clearly identifying failure modes such as "reluctance to act" and "overthinking collapse," it encourages the development of agents with better proactive planning, error recovery mechanisms, and more efficient tool utilization strategies.
*   **Standardization:** The unified execution harness supporting both code execution and function-calling APIs promotes fair and comparable evaluation across diverse agent implementations.

In conclusion, Agentic-MME provides a comprehensive and detailed framework for understanding what agentic capabilities truly contribute to multimodal intelligence, moving the field towards the development of MLLMs that are not just intelligent observers but also effective and reliable problem-solvers in dynamic, real-world environments.
overview:
## Understanding Agentic Multimodal Intelligence

The evolution of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has moved beyond static interpretation of text and images. Modern AI systems are increasingly expected to act as "agents"—active problem-solvers that do not just process the information given but actively seek out what is missing. This shift involves two primary dimensions: Visual Expansion, where a model manipulates an image (cropping, enhancing, or rotating) to uncover hidden details, and Knowledge Expansion, where it performs open-web searches to verify facts or acquire new information.

![Figure 1: Comparison of task difficulty levels in Agentic-MME, from single visual operations to complex, intertwined reasoning involving both vision and knowledge expansion.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03016v1/x1.png)

However, as MLLMs become more agentic, evaluating them becomes significantly more difficult. Traditional benchmarks often focus solely on whether a model reaches the correct final answer. This "outcome-based" assessment is limited because it cannot distinguish between a model that reasoned correctly and one that simply made a lucky guess. Furthermore, it fails to diagnose why a model failed: was it a failure in planning, a mistake in using a tool, or an inability to perceive the evidence?

To address these challenges, the Agentic-MME benchmark introduces a process-verified framework. It evaluates the synergistic application of visual and knowledge expansion in agentic MLLMs across 418 real-world tasks. By implementing detailed, step-wise checkpoints, the benchmark provides a diagnostic look into the underlying reasoning process of multimodal agents.

## Limitations of Current Evaluation Frameworks

Prior to this work, evaluating the agentic capabilities of multimodal models suffered from three major shortcomings. First, there was a lack of unified tool integration. Benchmarks typically treated visual tools (like image cropping) and knowledge tools (like web search) as separate entities. In real-world scenarios, however, these tools must be used in coordination. An agent might need to crop an obscure logo in an image before searching the web to identify the brand it represents.

Second, the synergy between visual and knowledge expansion remained largely unexplored. Most existing tests do not require the deep coupling of these two capabilities. The "intertwined" tasks that define high-level multimodal intelligence—where neither vision nor search alone is sufficient—were missing from the evaluation landscape.

Finally, the absence of process verification made it nearly impossible to audit the model's behavior. If a model fails to answer a question about a small, blurry object in a high-resolution image, current benchmarks cannot tell if the model failed to recognize it needed to zoom in, if it zoomed into the wrong area, or if it correctly zoomed in but still couldn't identify the object. Agentic-MME solves this by recording and auditing every intermediate state an agent produces.

## The Agentic-MME Methodology

The benchmark is built on a unified tool-augmented interface that provides agents with two main types of resources:

1.  **Visual Operations (13 tools):** These include `crop`, `rotate`, `enhance`, `grayscale`, `blur`, and `edge_detect`. These allow the model to actively manipulate the visual input to extract localized or processed cues.
2.  **Open-Web Retrieval (4 tools):** These include `google_search`, `google_lens_search`, `fetch_webpage`, and `download_image`. These enable the model to access the vast repository of human knowledge on the internet.

### Task Stratification by Difficulty
Agentic-MME categorizes tasks into three levels based on the complexity of interaction required:

*   **Level 1 (Visual Expansion Focus):** These tasks require a single, decisive visual operation. For example, a model might need to crop a price tag on a grocery shelf to read the value correctly.
*   **Level 2 (Visual + Knowledge):** These involve multi-step workflows, typically combining visual manipulation with a search. A model might crop a sign to read a restaurant name and then search the web to find its operating hours.
*   **Level 3 (Synergistic Coupling):** These are the most challenging scenarios. They require iterative, interleaved execution of both visual and search tools. Solving these tasks often involves resolving severe visual ambiguity through hypothesis-testing loops, where visual hints and multi-hop web searches are used for cross-validation.

### Process-Level Metrics
To move beyond final-answer accuracy ($Acc$), Agentic-MME introduces several process-aware metrics:

*   **$V\text{-axis}$ (Visual Verification):** This measures how well the agent uses visual tools. It is split into $V_{\text{tool}}$ (did the agent call the right tool at the right time?) and $V_{\text{true}}$ (did the resulting image actually contain the evidence needed?).
*   **$S\text{-axis}$ (Search Verification):** This assesses the agent's search strategy, including the relevance of its keywords and the correctness of intermediate facts it retrieved.
*   **Overthink:** This metric quantifies efficiency by comparing the agent's trajectory to a human's minimal path. It is defined as:
$$\text{Overthink} = \frac{\max(0, C_{\text{agent}} - C_{\text{human}})}{C_{\text{human}} + 1}$$
where $C_{\text{agent}}$ and $C_{\text{human}}$ represent the number of interactions for the agent and the human, respectively.

## Data Collection and Quality Assurance

The creation of Agentic-MME involved a rigorous "Model-in-the-Loop Backward Drafting" protocol. Researchers started with high-resolution, complex images and used advanced MLLMs (like Gemini 1.5 Pro) to identify details that were difficult to perceive without active manipulation. Once a candidate task was identified, human annotators meticulously documented a full reference trajectory.

![Figure 2: The data collection pipeline, emphasizing human-model co-verification and granular step-wise annotation.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03016v1/x2.png)

For every step in the trajectory, the annotators recorded the intent, the specific tool used, the intermediate visual or textual evidence, and the expected answer at that checkpoint. Quality control was maintained through consensus auditing and "Step-wise Oracle Testing," where models were guided through human paths to ensure that the evidence was indeed perceptible and the failure modes were strictly related to planning or execution.

## Key Findings and Model Analysis

The evaluation of state-of-the-art models on Agentic-MME revealed a significant performance gap between AI and humans. While humans achieved an overall accuracy of 93.8%, even the most capable model, Gemini 1.5 Pro, scored only 56.3%. On the most difficult Level 3 tasks, the gap widened further (82.3% for humans vs. 33.3% for the best model).

### Structured APIs vs. Code Generation
The benchmark tested models in two interaction modes: **Atomic (Atm)** mode, where models use structured function-calling APIs, and **Code Generation (Gen)** mode, where models write Python code to perform operations. The results showed that structured APIs are currently more reliable. In Gen mode, models often struggled with syntax and program composition, leading to lower visual tool utilization. For example, GPT-4o in Gen mode showed a visual tool invocation rate ($V_{\text{tool}}$) of only 7.6%, but this jumped to over 70% when switched to the simpler Atm mode.

### Open-Source vs. Closed-Source
Closed-source models consistently outperformed open-source ones, particularly in their ability to plan and execute searches. Analysis of the $S\text{-axis}$ scores showed that many open-source models failed to formulate useful search queries or extract relevant information from search results, leading to $S\text{-axis}$ scores below 5%.

### The "Overthinking" Problem
A unique finding of this research is the tendency for models to "overthink." While a human might find an answer in two or three steps, models often enter redundant loops, calling tools repeatedly without making progress. Gemini 1.5 Pro was found to be the most successful because it balanced exploration with reliable execution, whereas other models either failed to act ("reluctance to act") or explored aimlessly without achieving high accuracy.

## Diagnostic Failure Modes

Agentic-MME identifies seven distinct failure modes that hinder multimodal agents:

1.  **Reluctance to Act:** The model guesses an answer based on passive observation rather than using a tool to find the required evidence.
2.  **Overthinking Collapse:** The agent becomes trapped in redundant, circular tool-invocation loops.
3.  **Unfaithful Visual Tool Use:** The agent uses a tool (like `crop`) but targets the wrong region of the image.
4.  **Tool-Misexecution:** Failures in syntax or API parameterization, common in code-generation modes.
5.  **Bad Search Query:** Formulating queries that are too vague or irrelevant to the task.
6.  **Missing Search/Visual Tools:** The agent fails to recognize that it needs to search or manipulate the image to find the answer.
7.  **Post-Visual Perception Deficit:** The model correctly uses a tool to isolate evidence but fails to correctly interpret the newly processed image.

![Figure 3: Heatmap of failure modes across different models and task difficulty levels, showing that missing visual tool use is a primary bottleneck.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03016v1/x7.png)

## Significance of the Work

The Agentic-MME benchmark serves as a critical diagnostic tool for the AI research community. By pinpointing exactly where multimodal agents fail—be it in the planning stage, the execution of a tool, or the final perception of processed data—it provides a roadmap for future development.

The work highlights that true multimodal intelligence is not just about having more parameters or better vision encoders; it is about the coordination of disparate skills. The benchmark pushes the field toward "active" intelligence, where models are trained not just to describe what they see, but to strategically manipulate their environment and retrieve external knowledge to solve complex, real-world problems. By providing a process-verified evaluation, Agentic-MME ensures that progress in the field is measured by robust reasoning rather than sophisticated pattern matching.

keyInsights: Even leading MLLM agents exhibit a substantial performance disparity compared to human solvers, particularly in multi-step planning and reliable tool execution for complex, intertwined tasks.
Open-source models demonstrate significant limitations in robust search planning and effective knowledge retrieval, hindering their ability to leverage external information.
While flexible, code generation interfaces currently impose a higher cognitive load on models, leading to less reliable tool execution compared to structured function-calling APIs.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal benchmarks lacked integrated evaluation of visual manipulation and open-web search tools, often treating them as separate functionalities.
The synergistic interplay between visual and knowledge expansion in complex, real-world scenarios remained largely unexplored.
Evaluations predominantly focused on final answer correctness, offering limited insight into intermediate steps, tool application accuracy, or specific failure modes.
results: The top-performing model, Gemini 3 Pro (Atm mode), achieved 56.3% overall accuracy and 33.3% on Level-3 tasks, significantly trailing human performance (93.8% overall, 82.3% on Level-3).
Open-source models demonstrated lower performance, with some like Qwen3 VL-235B reaching 10.1% on Level-3 tasks, and exhibiting S-scores below 5% for effective knowledge expansion in certain cases.

Structured function-calling (Atm) mode consistently matched or surpassed code generation (Gen) mode in tool invocation and accuracy, exemplified by a model's V_tool increasing from 7.6% to over 70% when switching modes.
solution: Developed Agentic-MME, a benchmark featuring 418 real-world tasks across 6 domains requiring the combined use of 13 visual operations and 4 open-web retrieval tools.
Designed tasks with increasing difficulty, culminating in Level 3 scenarios demanding iterative, interleaved execution and synergistic coupling of visual and knowledge expansion.

Implemented a process-verified evaluation methodology with granular step-wise human reference trajectories, dual-axis metrics (V_tool, V_true, S-axis), and an "Overthink" metric to diagnose intermediate failures and efficiency.
summary.summary: Agentic-MME introduces a process-verified benchmark for assessing how multimodal large language models coordinate visual manipulation and open-web search tools to solve complex, real-world problems. Evaluation reveals a substantial performance gap, with leading models reaching 56.3% overall accuracy compared to human 93.8%, especially struggling with multi-step planning and reliable tool execution in intricately intertwined tasks.

title: Agentic-MME: What Agentic Capability Really Brings to Multimodal Intelligence?
